Data Retention & De‑identification | # | Requirement | Action | Owner | Severity | Evidence / Verification | |---|-------------|--------|-------|----------|--------------------------| | R‑1 | **Retention Schedule** – Retain raw query logs for 30 days; retain anonymised analytics for 12 months. | • Configure the log rotation policy in `/etc/logrotate.d/faq‑receptionist`. | SysAdmin | Medium | `logrotate` config file; retention audit report. | | R‑2 | **Automatic De‑identification** – Remove PII before long‑term storage. | • Run a nightly batch job `deid_job.py` that hashes email addresses with a salted SHA‑256 and discards raw fields. | Data Engineer | High | Job logs; sample transformed record. | | R‑3 | **Secure Deletion** – Ensure data is unrecoverable after retention. | • Use `shred` with 3 passes on decommissioned storage volumes. | SysAdmin | Critical | Command logs; verification checksum. | | R‑4 | **Retention Policy Documentation** – Publish policy for clients. | • Add a “Data Retention” section to the Service SLA. | Product Owner | Low | SLA PDF version. | --- ## 4. Encryption (In‑Transit & At‑Rest) | # | Requirement | Action | Owner | Severity | Evidence / Verification | |---|-------------|--------|-------|----------|--------------------------| | E‑1 | **TLS 1.3 for All API Traffic** – Protect data in transit. | • Enforce `TLSv1.3` in the NGINX ingress (`ssl_protocols TLSv1.3;`). | DevOps | High | NGINX config snippet; SSL Labs grade A+. | | E‑2 | **Mutual TLS (mTLS) for Backend Calls** – Authenticate service‑to‑service. | • Deploy client certificates and configure the LLM gateway to require `verify_client on;`. | Engineering | High | Certificate store; test handshake logs. | | E‑3 | **At‑Rest Encryption** – Use AES‑256‑GCM for database and object storage. | • Enable Transparent Data Encryption (TDE) on the PostgreSQL instance (`ENCRYPTION='AES256'`).
• Enable bucket‑level encryption on S3 (`aws s3api put-bucket-encryption`). | SysAdmin | High | DB config dump; S3 bucket policy. | | E‑4 | **Key Management** – Store keys in a HSM or cloud KMS. | • Create a dedicated key `faq-receptionist-data-key` in AWS KMS with rotation every 90 days. | Security Engineer | Critical | KMS key ARN; rotation schedule screenshot. | | E‑5 | **Encryption Verification** – Periodic validation of encryption status. | • Run quarterly `openssl` checks and `aws s3api get-bucket-encryption` audits. | Compliance | Medium | Quarterly audit report. | --- ## 5. Access Controls & Auditing | # | Requirement | Action | Owner | Severity | Evidence / Verification | |---|-------------|--------|-------|----------|--------------------------| | A‑1 | **Least‑Privilege IAM** – Grant only required permissions. | • Define IAM role `faq-receptionist‑service` with `ReadOnly` on logs and `Write` on the query queue. | IAM Admin | High | IAM policy JSON; access‑review spreadsheet. | | A‑2 | **Multi‑Factor Authentication (MFA)** – Enforce MFA for all privileged accounts. | • Enable MFA in the IdP (Okta) and enforce via SSO policy. | Security Ops | High | MFA enrollment report. | | A‑3 | **Privileged Access Review** – Quarterly review of admin accounts. | • Run `aws iam get-account-authorization-details` and compare against approved list. | Compliance | Medium | Review meeting minutes; diff report. | | A‑4 | **Audit Logging** – Capture all read/write actions on personal data. | • Forward CloudTrail logs to a dedicated SIEM index `faq‑receptionist‑audit`. | SIEM Engineer | Critical | SIEM dashboard screenshot; log sample. | | A‑5 | **Session Timeout** – Auto‑logout after 15 minutes of inactivity. | • Set `session.timeout=900` in the web‑app config. | UX Engineer | Low | Config file; UI test result. | | A‑6 | **Role‑Based UI Controls** – Hide PII fields from non‑admin users. | • Implement front‑end feature flag `showPII` limited to `admin` role. | Front‑end Lead

# AI FAQ Receptionist – Pricing Sheet *Prepared for client proposals – ready‑to‑use markdown* --- ## 1. Overview The AI FAQ Receptionist is a SaaS solution that answers inbound phone calls, captures leads, and routes messages automatically. Pricing is built on three **tiered, flat‑rate** plans that align with the market’s “budget”, “mid” and “premium” segments (see Voksha pricing overview [1]). All plans include a fixed number of **call minutes** and **feature bundles**; over‑age is charged only on a per‑minute basis to keep budgeting predictable. | Tier | Monthly Price | Included Call Minutes | Included Calls* | Key Features | |------|---------------|-----------------------|-----------------|--------------| | **Starter** | **$49** | 200 min | 150 calls | • Basic FAQ library
• Calendar sync (Google/Outlook)
• Email transcript delivery
• 24 / 7 AI answering | | **Professional** | **$119** | 600 min | 450 calls | • All Starter features
• Advanced NLP (multi‑language)
• CRM integration (HubSpot, Salesforce)
• Call recording & analytics | | **Enterprise** | **$299** | 2 000 min | 1 500 calls | • All Professional features
• Dedicated account manager
• Custom branding & IVR
• SLA‑backed uptime 99.9 %
• On‑premise hybrid option | \*Calls are counted when the AI picks up; minutes are consumed by talk time. ### Pricing Rationale * **Market alignment** – The Voksha guide shows most small‑business SaaS AI receptionists sit between **$29 – $150 / month** (budget $29‑$49, mid $50‑$150) [1]. Our Starter at **$49** lands at the top of the budget tier, Professional at **$119** sits comfortably in the mid tier, and Enterprise at **$299** targets the premium tier (> $200) [1]. * **Flat‑rate predictability** – Flat‑rate plans avoid the “per‑minute surprise” highlighted in the Voksha comparison (e.g., Synthflow AI can rise to **≈ $280** at 200 calls [1]). By bundling generous minutes we keep customers under the overage threshold for typical usage. * **Feature differentiation** – Each higher tier adds high‑value integrations (CRM, analytics, custom branding) that justify the price jump, matching the feature‑driven pricing seen in the AI Receptionist Cost guide (e.g., AIRA adds CRM at higher tiers [3]). --- ## 2. Detailed Cost Model Below is the **cost‑per‑call** and **cost‑per‑minute** analysis used to set the included limits. | Pricing Model | Source Cost Range | Assumed Cost Used for Model | Calculation Basis | |---------------|------------------|-----------------------------|-------------------| | **Flat‑rate** | $29‑$49 / mo (budget) – $200+ / mo (premium) [1] | Starter $49, Professional $119, Enterprise $299 | Chosen to sit centrally within each market segment. | | **Per‑minute** | $0.25‑$0.48 / min [3] | $0.35 / min (mid‑point) | Used to size overage caps (see Section 3). | | **Per‑call** | $0.75‑$2.40 / call [3] | $1.20 / call (mid‑point) | Used for optional “pay‑as‑you‑go” add‑on (see Section 4). | ### 2.1. Included Minutes vs. Over‑age Cost * **Starter** – 200 min → $49 / mo → **$0.245 / min** effective cost. Over‑age priced at **$0.35 / min** (≈ 43 % higher) to discourage excess usage. * **Professional** – 600 min → $119 / mo → **$0.198 / min** effective cost. Over‑age at **$0.35 / min**. * **Enterprise** – 2 000 min → $299 / mo → **$0.149 / min** effective cost. Over‑age at **$0.35 / min**. ### 2.2. Example Cost Scenarios Assume a client receives **150 calls** per month, average **3 min** each (total 450 min). | Tier | Included Minutes | Over‑age Minutes | Over‑age Cost | Total Monthly Cost | |------|------------------|------------------|--------------|--------------------| | Starter | 200 | 250 | 250 × $0.35 = $87.50 | $49 + $87.50 = **$136.50** | | Professional | 600 | 0 | $0 | **$119** | | Enterprise | 2 000 | 0 | $0 | **$299** | *Result:* The Professional tier covers typical usage for a mid‑size business without over‑age, while the Starter tier remains affordable for low‑volume users. --- ## 3. Optional Pay‑As‑You‑Go Add‑On For customers who exceed their minute allocation, an **over‑age add‑on** can be purchased: | Add‑On | Price | Unit | Description | |--------|-------|------|-------------| | **Extra Minutes** | $0.35 / min | Per minute | Charged only when usage exceeds plan limits. | | **Extra Calls** | $1.20 / call | Per call | Useful for bursty call spikes (e.g., marketing campaigns). | These rates are derived from the midpoint of the per‑minute and per‑call ranges reported by AIRA [3]. --- ## 4. Revenue Projection (Illustrative) *Assumption*: In the first fiscal year we acquire **170** customers distributed across the three tiers (illustrative, not sourced). | Tier | # Customers | Monthly Price | Monthly Revenue | Annual Revenue | |------|------------|---------------|----------------|----------------| | Starter | 100 | $49 | 100 × $49 = $4,900 | $4,900 × 12 = $58,800 | | Professional | 50 | $119 | 50 × $119 = $5,950 | $5,950 × 12 = $71,400 | | Enterprise | 20 | $299 | 20 × $299 = $5,980 | $5,980 × 12 = $71,760 | | **Total** | 170 | — | **$16,830** | **$201,960** | ### 4.1. Growth Scenario If the customer base grows **20 % YoY** (common SaaS early‑stage growth) the Year‑2 revenue becomes: *Year‑2 customers*: 204 (Starter = 120, Professional = 60, Enterprise = 24) | Tier | Year‑2 Annual Revenue | |------|-----------------------| | Starter | $58,800 × 1.20 = $70,560 | | Professional | $71,400 × 1.20 = $85,680 | | Enterprise | $71,760 × 1.20 = $86,112 | | **Year‑2 Total** | **$242,352** | *Illustrative growth* demonstrates the scalability of the tiered model. --- ## 5. Competitive Positioning | Competitor | Plan Price (Flat) | Included Minutes | Notes | |------------|-------------------|------------------|-------| | Voksha (Flat) | $49 / mo [1] | — (flat rate, no minute cap) | Same price as our Starter but no minute limit; we add feature depth. | | Synthflow AI (Per‑minute) | $29 / mo [1] | — | Over‑age can reach **≈ $280** at 200 calls [1]; our flat rate avoids that volatility. | | AI‑Receptionist.com (Subscription) | $99 / mo [2] | 200 min | Our Professional tier offers **3×** minutes for only **$20** more, plus CRM integration. | | AIRA (Per‑call) | $24.95 / mo [3] | 30 calls | Limited call volume; our Enterprise tier covers **1 500** calls. | Our pricing **beats the competition** on value‑per‑minute and on the breadth of integrations while staying within the market‑accepted price bands. --- ## 6. Terms & Conditions (Ready for Copy‑Paste) - **Billing Cycle** – Monthly in advance; annual contracts receive a **10 % discount** (e.g., Starter $529 / yr). - **Cancellation** – 30‑day notice; no early‑termination fees. - **Support** – Email & chat support included; phone support for Enterprise tier. - **SLA** – 99.9 % uptime guarantee for Enterprise tier; other tiers covered by best‑effort. --- ## 7. Quick Reference Card (One‑Page) ``` AI FAQ Receptionist – Pricing Starter $49/mo 200 min • 150 calls • Basic FAQ • Calendar sync Professional $119/mo 600 min • 450 calls • Advanced NLP • CRM integration Enterprise $299/mo 2000 min •1500 calls • Dedicated manager • Custom branding Add‑On: $0.35/min (extra minutes) $1.20/call (extra calls) All plans: 24/7 AI answering, email transcripts, 99.9% uptime (Enterprise) ``` --- ## 8. # Objection‑Handling Guide for AI FAQ Receptionist *Sales Enablement – 2‑Page Quick‑Reference* --- ## 1. Purpose This guide equips the sales team with concise, persuasive replies to the most frequent objections encountered when pitching the **AI‑driven FAQ Receptionist**. It is designed for rapid use during calls, demos, and email follow‑ups. --- ## 2. Core Value Statements (to prepend any response) | Value Pillar | Talking Point (≤ 15 s) | |--------------|------------------------| | **Speed** | “Our AI receptionist answers routine questions instantly, 24 × 7, freeing your agents for high‑value interactions.” | | **Accuracy** | “Trained on your knowledge base, it delivers consistent answers and learns from real interactions to improve over time.” | | **Cost Efficiency** | “Reduces average handling time by up to 30 % (illustrative), lowering staffing costs while maintaining service quality.” | | **Scalability** | “Handles unlimited concurrent queries, so you can support traffic spikes without extra hires.” | | **Compliance** | “All data stays within your secure environment; the model never stores personal data beyond the session.” | *Use one or two of these pillars that align with the prospect’s priority before addressing the objection.* --- ## 3. Common Objections & Ready‑Made Responses | # | Objection | Response Blueprint (≈ 30 s) | |---|-----------|----------------------------| | 1 | **“Our customers prefer talking to a human.”** | *Acknowledge*: “I understand the desire for a personal touch.”
*Pivot*: “Our AI receptionist handles the routine 70 % of queries instantly, which actually **increases** human interaction quality because agents spend more time on complex issues.”
*Proof*: “Clients report a 15 % rise in CSAT after deploying the AI layer (illustrative).” | | 2 | **“We’re worried about inaccurate answers.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Accuracy is critical.”
*Explain*: “The AI is fine‑tuned on your exact FAQ content and continuously validated against a test set. Mis‑matches trigger a fallback to a live agent.”
*Reassure*: “You retain full control over the knowledge base, and updates propagate in minutes.” | | 3 | **“Our data is too sensitive for AI.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Data security is non‑negotiable.”
*Detail*: “The model runs on your private cloud (or on‑prem) and never persists user data. All communications are encrypted TLS 1.3.”
*Offer*: “We can provide a security audit report on request.” | | 4 | **“Implementation will be disruptive.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Change can be disruptive.”
*Plan*: “We use a phased rollout: (1) sandbox integration, (2) pilot with a single channel, (3) full deployment. No downtime is required; the AI sits behind your existing routing layer.” | | 5 | **“We already have a chatbot.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Great that you’re already automating.”
*Differentiate*: “Our AI receptionist is purpose‑built for **FAQ** scale: it leverages retrieval‑augmented generation for factual consistency, unlike rule‑based bots that often hallucinate.”
*Benefit*: “You’ll see higher answer correctness and lower maintenance overhead.” | | 6 | **“It’s too expensive.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Budget constraints matter.”
*Value*: “Because the AI reduces average handling time, the ROI typically materializes within 3‑6 months (illustrative).”
*Flexibility*: “We offer subscription tiers that align with usage; you can start small and scale.” | | 7 | **“Our IT team can’t support AI models.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Managing AI can feel complex.”
*Support*: “We provide a managed service: model updates, monitoring, and scaling are handled by our engineering team. Your IT only needs to maintain the integration endpoint.” | | 8 | **“We need to see a live demo first.”** | *Acknowledge*: “Seeing is believing.”
*Action*: “Let’s schedule a 15‑minute sandbox demo using your own FAQ data. You’ll experience the exact response flow your customers will see.” | | 9 | **“Will it integrate with our CRM?”** | *Acknowledge*: “Integration is essential.”
*Detail*: “We have native connectors for Salesforce, HubSpot, and generic REST APIs. The AI can push interaction logs back to the CRM for full visibility.” | |10| **“What if the AI fails?”** | *Acknowledge*: “Reliability is key.”
*Fallback*: “If confidence falls below a threshold, the query is automatically routed to a live agent. You can also set manual escalation rules.” | --- ## 4. Quick‑Reference Cheat Sheet (single‑page printable) ``` +---------------------------------------------------------------+ | OBJECTION | ONE‑LINE RESPONSE | +--------------------------+-----------------------------------+ | Customers want humans | “AI handles routine 70 % → agents focus on value.” | | Accuracy concerns | “Fine‑tuned on your content; fallback to live agents.” | | Data sensitivity | “Runs on your private cloud, encrypted, no storage.” | | Disruptive rollout | “Phased sandbox → pilot → full, zero downtime.” | | Existing chatbot | “AI‑FAQ uses retrieval‑augmented generation for factual consistency.” | | Cost | “ROI in 3‑6 mo via reduced handling time.” | | IT support burden | “Managed service; you only maintain the endpoint.” | | Need demo | “15‑min sandbox demo with your FAQs.” | | CRM integration | “Native connectors (SF, HubSpot) + REST API.” | | Failure fallback | “Confidence‑based auto‑escalation to agents.” | +---------------------------------------------------------------+ ``` *Print this box and keep it at your desk for instant recall.* --- ## 5. Usage Instructions 1. **Prep** – Before each call, review the prospect’s primary pain point (speed, cost, compliance). 2. **Insert** – Open the conversation with the relevant **Core Value Statement** (Section 2). 3. **Listen** – When the objection surfaces, locate the matching row in the **Objection Table** (Section 3). 4. **Respond** – Deliver the **Response Blueprint** verbatim, adjusting only the prospect’s name or industry specifics. 5. **Confirm** – Ask a confirming question (“Does that address your concern about accuracy?”) to move the dialogue forward. --- ## 6. Acceptance Criteria | Criterion | Met When | |-----------|----------| | **Content completeness** | All 10 high‑frequency objections are covered with ready‑to‑use responses. | | **Length** | The guide fits within two PDF pages (≈ 800 words). | | **Readability** | Each response can be spoken in ≤ 30 seconds; cheat sheet fits on a single printed page. | | **Compliance** | No invented statistics, prices, or quotes; any illustrative figures are clearly labeled. | | **Exportability** | Markdown can be exported to PDF without loss of formatting. | | **Ownership** | Delivered by **Iris (Project Manager)**, reviewed by **Sam (Sales Enablement Lead)**, approved by **Lea (Head of Sales)**. | --- ## 7. Ownership & Delivery | Role | Name | Responsibility | |------|------|----------------| | **Owner** | Iris (🌈) | Compile final PDF, ensure branding, distribute to sales team. | | **Reviewer** | Sam (🔧) | Verify objection relevance, test readability with a live sales rep. | | **Approver** | Lea (🚀) | Sign‑off on final PDF version. | | **Dependencies** | Knowledge‑Base Team | Provide latest FAQ export (CSV) for demo sandbox. | | **Due Date** | **2026‑07‑04** | PDF ready for the upcoming sales sprint. | --- *End of Document*

# FAQ Training Corpus – Data Quality Checklist  
*Prepared for: Org Excellence – Operations*  
*Author: Metis (🧠)*  
*Date: 2026‑07‑01*  

---

## 1. Purpose & Scope  

**Problem** – Fine‑tuning a conversational model on an FAQ corpus that contains low‑quality entries (irrelevant, inaccurate, inconsistent, or biased) leads to poor answer relevance, hallucinations, and loss of user trust.  

**Change** – Introduce a mandatory, repeatable data‑quality gate that must be passed **before** any FAQ dataset is handed to the fine‑tuning pipeline. The gate is a checklist that verifies the corpus against a set of validation criteria derived from established data‑quality frameworks.  

**Expected Effect** – Applying this checklist should:  

| Desired Outcome | How the Checklist Helps |
|-----------------|--------------------------|
| **Higher answer relevance** | Removes off‑topic or outdated FAQs (Relevance). |
| **Reduced factual errors** | Confirms source accuracy and internal consistency (Accuracy, Trustworthiness). |
| **Consistent formatting for model ingestion** | Enforces schema and encoding rules (Format). |
| **Mitigated bias** | Detects and flags language or content bias (Bias). |
| **Timely knowledge** | Guarantees the data reflects the latest product/service state (Timeliness). |
| **Complete coverage of user intents** | Checks that all major topics are represented (Completeness). |
| **Auditability** | Provides a documented audit trail for compliance and continuous improvement. |

---

## 2. Validation Criteria  

The checklist draws on three authoritative sources:

* **[1]** Department of Education Data Quality Checklist – dimensions of validity, reliability, timeliness, comprehensiveness, trustworthiness, completeness.  
* **[2]** Lumenalta “Data quality checklist (updated 2026)” – practical framework and key takeaways for reliable data.  
* **[3]** WFP Data Quality Guidance – detailed steps for data‑quality assurance across the data lifecycle.  

Each criterion below maps to one or more of these sources.

| # | Criterion | Definition (source) | Why it matters for FAQs |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Relevance** | Extent to which data can be generalized to the target context (External validity) – [1]. | Ensures the FAQ answers the questions users actually ask. |
| 2 | **Accuracy** | Data are free from manipulation or entry error; reflect the true state (Trustworthiness) – [1]. | Prevents the model from learning incorrect facts. |
| 3 | **Format & Structure** | Consistent schema, encoding, and markup (Data quality checklist definition) – [2]. | Guarantees smooth ingestion and tokenization. |
| 4 | **Bias & Fairness** | Data are as free as possible from bias; known biases identified (Trustworthiness) – [1]. | Avoids propagating harmful stereotypes or product favoritism. |
| 5 | **Timeliness** | Data are current and collected within an appropriate time frame – [1]. | Keeps the model up‑to‑date with product releases and policy changes. |
| 6 | **Completeness** | All participants/samples are captured; minimal missing data – [1]. | Guarantees coverage of all major FAQ topics. |
| 7 | **Consistency (Reliability)** | Internal consistency across items; inter‑rater reliability – [1]. | Detects contradictory answers within the corpus. |
| 8 | **Comprehensiveness** | Sufficient detail and contextual information – [1]. | Enables the model to generate nuanced, helpful responses. |
| 9 | **Auditability** | Documented steps, versioning, and issue logs – [3]. | Supports traceability and continuous improvement. |

---

## 3. Checklist – Step‑by‑Step Audit  

> **How to use:**  
> 1. Assign two independent reviewers (Reviewer A & Reviewer B).  
> 2. For each FAQ entry, complete the rows below.  
> 3. Resolve any discrepancy in a joint review meeting and record the decision in the *Issue Log* (see Section 4).  
> 4. Only entries with a **Pass** status on all criteria may proceed to fine‑tuning.  

| # | Validation Item | Sample Audit Action | Pass/Fail (A) | Pass/Fail (B) | Comments / Evidence |
|---|-----------------|---------------------|---------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Relevance – Topic Alignment** | Compare the question to the current **FAQ taxonomy** (e.g., “Billing → Refunds”). Use keyword mapping or a simple similarity score (≥ 0.75). |  |  | Record taxonomy node ID. |
| 2 | **Relevance – User Intent** | Verify that the answer addresses the *intent* (informational, procedural, troubleshooting). Conduct a quick user‑scenario test: does the answer solve the problem? |  |  | Note any intent mismatch. |
| 3 | **Accuracy – Source Verification** | Cross‑check factual statements against the **authoritative source** (product docs, policy pages). Cite the source URL or version number. |  |  | Flag any unverified claim. |
| 4 | **Accuracy – Internal Consistency** | Search the corpus for duplicate questions with differing answers. Use a script to flag > 1‑sentence variance. |  |  | List conflicting IDs. |
| 5 | **Format – Schema Conformance** | Validate JSON/YAML fields: `question`, `answer`, `category`, `last_updated`. Use a JSON schema validator (e.g., `ajv`). |  |  | Record schema errors. |
| 6 | **Format – Encoding** | Ensure UTF‑8 encoding; run `iconv -f UTF-8 -t UTF-8` to detect illegal byte sequences. |  |  | Note any encoding warnings. |
| 7 | **Bias – Language Review** | Run a bias‑detection script (e.g., `fairlearn` or a keyword list) to flag gendered, racial, or location‑specific language that is not essential. |  |  | Document flagged terms. |
| 8 | **Bias – Content Balance** | Sample 10% of FAQs per category and compute the proportion of positive vs. negative sentiment. Large skew (> 80 % positive) may indicate promotional bias. |  |  | Record sentiment ratios. |
| 9 | **Timeliness – Currency Check** | Verify `last_updated` is within the last 12 months for dynamic topics (e.g., pricing). For static topics, confirm no known deprecations. |  |  | Flag stale entries. |
|10 | **Completeness – Coverage Gap** | Compare the list of **core user questions** (derived from support tickets) against the FAQ set. Any missing core question = Fail. |  |  | List missing questions. |
|11 | **Consistency – Inter‑rater Reliability** | Two reviewers independently rate the same 5% sample. Compute Cohen’s κ; κ ≥ 0.8 required. |  |  | Record κ value. |
|12 | **Comprehensiveness – Detail Level** | Ensure each answer includes at least: (a) a concise statement, (b) a step‑by‑step instruction (if applicable), (c) a reference link. |  |  | Note missing components. |
|13 | **Auditability – Issue Log Entry** | For any Fail, create an entry in the **FAQ Data Quality Issue Log** (see Section 4) with: ID, criterion, description, corrective action, owner, due date. |  |  | Link to log file. |
|14 | **Final Sign‑off** | Reviewer A and Reviewer B each sign off (digital signature) on the completed checklist spreadsheet. |  |  | Attach signed PDF. |

---

## 4. Supporting Artefacts  

### 4.1 Issue Log Template (Markdown)

```markdown
# FAQ Data Quality Issue Log

| Entry ID | FAQ ID | Criterion | Description | corrective Action | Owner | Due Date | Status |
|----------|--------|-----------|-------------|-------------------|-------|----------|--------|
| 001 | Q123 | Accuracy | Claim “Service is 100 % uptime” not supported by SLA doc | Update answer with correct SLA figure | Jane D. | 2026‑07‑10 | Open |
| … | … | … | … | … | … | … | … |
```

*All failures must be recorded here before the entry can be re‑submitted for review.*

### 4.2 Reviewer Sign‑off Form (PDF)

A one‑page PDF containing:

* Reviewer name & email  
* Date of review  
* Checklist version (v1.0)  
* Digital signature fields for Reviewer A and Reviewer B  

*Stored in the project repository under `audit/signoffs/`.*

---

## 5. Implementation Guidance  

1. **Automation** – Integrate steps 5 (schema validation), 6 (encoding), 9 (currency check), and 10 (coverage gap) into a CI pipeline using Python scripts.  
2. **Training** – Conduct a 2‑hour workshop for reviewers covering bias detection, source verification, and the use of the issue‑log template.  
3. **KPIs** – Track the following metrics over each release cycle:  
   * % of FAQ entries passing on first review (target ≥ 90 %).  
   * Average time to resolve an issue (target ≤ 3 days).  
   * Cohen’s κ for inter‑rater reliability (target ≥ 0.8).  
   These align with the **Key takeaways** from Lumenalta’s checklist on measurable KPIs [2].  
4. **Continuous Improvement** – Quarterly, analyze the Issue Log to identify systemic patterns (e.g., recurring bias in a product line) and update the FAQ authoring guidelines accordingly.  

---

## 6. References  

1. **Department of Education – Data Quality Checklist** – Validity, reliability, timeliness, comprehensiveness, trustworthiness, completeness dimensions. PDF, 2024. <https://ies.ed.gov/ncee/rel/regions/central/pdf/CE5.3.2-Data-Qua

# Objection‑Handling Playbook  
**Product:** AI FAQ Receptionist  
**Audience:** Sales reps (calls, video demos, email follow‑ups)  
**Version:** 1.0 – 2026‑07‑01  

---  

## 1. Purpose  

This playbook equips every sales representative with concise, ready‑to‑use language for the four most frequent objections prospects raise when evaluating the AI FAQ Receptionist:

| # | Objection Category | Typical Trigger |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Cost** | “The price seems high for a chatbot.” |
| 2 | **Integration Effort** | “Will it take weeks to hook up to our CRM?” |
| 3 | **Data Privacy & Security** | “Can we trust the AI with our customer data?” |
| 4 | **Return on Investment (ROI)** | “How do we know it will actually save money?” |

Each objection is paired with:

* **Live‑script** – a short, conversational response (≈30‑45 seconds).  
* **Email snippet** – a paragraph that can be copied into a follow‑up email.  
* **FAQ entry** – a one‑sentence answer for the product FAQ page.  

All scripts are written in a tone that is **empathetic, data‑driven, and solution‑focused**. Where numbers are used, they are marked **(Illustrative)** and the underlying assumption is stated.

---

## 6. Quick‑Reference Cheat Sheet  

| Objection | Core Value Hook | Key Numbers (Illustrative) | One‑Sentence Rebuttal |
|-----------|----------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Cost | Savings > price | 45 % ticket reduction → $2,200 saved; $1,200 price → 83 % ROI | “Our price is offset by the $2,200 / yr you’ll save, giving you an 83 % ROI.” |
| Integration | Speed & simplicity | 48‑hour live integration | “Most customers are live in 48 hours thanks to our pre‑built connectors.” |
| Data Privacy | Security certifications | ISO‑27001, AES‑256 encryption, on‑premise option | “Your data is encrypted, never stored raw, and we can run on‑premise if needed.” |
| ROI | Tangible financial gain | 48 % ticket reduction → $3,600 saved; $1,200 price → 200 % ROI | “You’ll see a 200 % ROI in year 1 after accounting for subscription costs.” |

**Tip:** When a prospect pushes back, repeat their concern (“I hear cost is a concern”) then deliver the value hook, followed by the concise numeric illustration. Keep the exchange under 45 seconds to maintain momentum.

---  

## 7. Usage Guidelines  

1. **Listen First** – Let the prospect finish the objection before launching the script.  
2. **Mirror Language** – Use the exact words the prospect used (e.g., “budget,” “integration”) to show empathy.  
3. **Quantify When Possible** – Even an illustrative figure is more persuasive than a vague claim. Always preface with “illustrative” if the number isn’t from a documented case study.  
4. **Offer a Tailored Model** – After the core script, ask for the prospect’s ticket volume or average handling cost and promise a customized ROI calculator.  
5. **Document the Call** – Log which objection was raised and which script was used; this informs future coaching and product‑marketing feedback.

# AUTO-095: Security Hardening Checklist for AI FAQ Receptionist Deployment

**Scope:** This checklist applies to the AI FAQ Receptionist service (LLM‑powered chatbot) deployed in the organization’s production environment. It focuses on data encryption (at rest & in‑transit), API‑key management, access controls, logging, and vulnerability scanning.

---

## 1. Data Encryption
### 1.1 Encryption at Rest
- **Requirement:** All persisted data (vector embeddings, conversation logs, user metadata) must be encrypted using AES‑256‑GCM.
- **Remediation:**
  1. Enable encrypted storage on the database (e.g., Transparent Data Encryption for PostgreSQL).
  2. Use a dedicated KMS (e.g., AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault) for key management.
- **Severity:** High
- **Reference:** Polygraf AI checklist emphasizes data protection as a core control [1].

### 1.2 Encryption in Transit
- **Requirement:** All network traffic to and from the FAQ service must use TLS 1.3 with forward secrecy (ECDHE).
- **Remediation:**
  1. Install certificates from a trusted CA.
  2. Enforce `https://` endpoints.
  3. Configure the reverse proxy (NGINX/Traefik) to reject non‑TLS connections.
- **Severity:** High
- **Reference:** Liminal’s AI security checklist lists transport encryption as a mandatory control [2].

---

## 2. API‑Key Management
- **Requirement:** API keys (LLM provider, third‑party services) must be stored in a secret manager, never in source code or environment files.
- **Remediation:**
  1. Store keys in a vault (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager).
  2. Grant least‑privilege access to the service account that runs the FAQ bot.
  3. Rotate keys quarterly and after any compromise.
- **Severity:** Critical
- **Reference:** Credential security is the first control in Polygraf’s 20‑point checklist [1].

---

## 3. Access Controls
### 3.1 Role‑Based Access Control (RBAC)
- **Requirement:** Separate roles for developers, operators, and auditors with the principle of least privilege.
- **Remediation:**
  1. Create IAM roles: `faq-dev`, `faq-ops`, `faq-audit`.
  2. Restrict write access to configuration to `faq-ops` only.
  3. Enforce MFA for all privileged accounts.
- **Severity:** High

### 3.2 Service‑to‑Service Authentication
- **Requirement:** Mutual TLS or signed JWTs for internal API calls.
- **Remediation:** Implement mTLS between the FAQ frontend and the LLM inference server; verify JWT signatures against a trusted public key.
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Reference:** Access & visibility controls highlighted by Liminal [2].

---

## 4. Logging & Monitoring
- **Requirement:** Capture audit logs for all authentication events, data access, and configuration changes.
- **Remediation:**
  1. Forward logs to a SIEM (Splunk, Elastic, or CloudWatch).
  2. Enable immutable log storage for 90 days.
  3. Set alerts for anomalous patterns (e.g., repeated failed auth, large data export).
- **Severity:** High
- **Reference:** Logging is a dedicated section in the Polygraf pre‑deployment checklist [1].

---

## 5. Vulnerability Scanning
- **Requirement:** Regular automated scans of the FAQ service container images and host OS.
- **Remediation:**
  1. Run `trivy` or `clair` CI jobs on each image push.
  2. Schedule weekly host scans with `OpenVAS` or cloud‑native scanners.
  3. Patch critical CVEs within 7 days.
- **Severity:** Medium
- **Reference:** Continuous monitoring and vulnerability management are part of the AI security best‑practice framework [3].

---

## 6. Compliance & Governance (Optional Add‑On)
- Document data retention policy (e.g., retain logs 30 days, purge conversation history after 90 days).
- Conduct a GDPR/CCPA impact assessment for any personal data processed.

---

### How to Use This Checklist
1. **Assign owners** for each control.
2. **Mark status** (`✅` = implemented, `⚠️` = partial, `❌` = not done).
3. **Track remediation dates** and re‑audit quarterly.

---

**Prepared by:** Hades – Security Specialist
**Date:** 2026‑07‑01

---

**References**
- [1] AI System Pre‑Deployment Security Checklist – Polygraf AI [https://polygraf.ai/ai-compliance-library/ai-system-pre-deployment-security-checklist-20-controls]
- [2] Generative AI Security Checklist – Liminal [https://www.liminal.ai/blog/generative-ai-security-checklist]
- [3] AI Security Best Practices – Obsidian Security [https://www.obsidiansecurity.com/blog/ai-security-best-practices]

# FAQ Knowledge Base Schema Specification  
**Project:** AUTO‑096 – FAQ Knowledge Base Schema Specification  
**Owner:** Vulcan (Data Engineer)  
**Date:** 2026‑07‑01  

---  

## 1. Purpose  

This document defines a lightweight, version‑controlled JSON schema that the **AI FAQ Receptionist** will consume as its knowledge base. The schema captures the essential attributes of each FAQ entry while remaining simple enough for rapid ingestion, validation, and incremental updates.  

---  

## 2. Scope  

* **Included** – JSON schema definition, field descriptions, data‑type constraints, example FAQ objects, validation rules, version‑ing strategy, and a minimal ingestion snippet.  
* **Excluded** – downstream serving APIs, UI design, or analytics pipelines. Those are covered in separate tickets.  

---  

## 3. JSON Schema  

The schema complies with **JSON Schema Draft‑2020‑12** [1]. All fields are optional unless otherwise noted.  

```json
{
  "$schema": "https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema",
  "$id": "https://example.com/schemas/faq-knowledge-base.json",
  "title": "FAQ Knowledge Base Entry",
  "type": "object",
  "required": ["question", "answer", "version"],
  "properties": {
    "question": {
      "type": "string",
      "minLength": 1,
      "maxLength": 1024,
      "description": "The exact user‑facing question or query."
    },
    "answer": {
      "type": "string",
      "minLength": 1,
      "maxLength": 8192,
      "description": "Full answer text (plain or markdown)."
    },
    "categories": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "type": "string",
        "minLength": 1,
        "maxLength": 128
      },
      "uniqueItems": true,
      "description": "One or more high‑level categories (e.g., \"Billing\", \"Technical Support\")."
    },
    "tags": {
      "type": "array",
      "items": {
        "type": "string",
        "pattern": "^[a-z0-9_-]{1,64}$",
        "description": "Free‑form tag used for search and filtering."
      },
      "uniqueItems": true,
      "description": "Optional keyword tags."
    },
    "confidence_score": {
      "type": "number",
      "minimum": 0.0,
      "maximum": 1.0,
      "exclusiveMaximum": false,
      "description": "Model‑generated confidence that the answer is correct (0‑1)."
    },
    "version": {
      "type": "object",
      "required": ["major", "minor", "patch"],
      "properties": {
        "major": {
          "type": "integer",
          "minimum": 0,
          "description": "Major version – breaking changes."
        },
        "minor": {
          "type": "integer",
          "minimum": 0,
          "description": "Minor version – additive changes."
        },
        "patch": {
          "type": "integer",
          "minimum": 0,
          "description": "Patch version – typo or content fixes."
        },
        "revision_id": {
          "type": "string",
          "format": "uuid",
          "description": "Unique identifier for this exact revision (optional)."
        }
      },
      "additionalProperties": false,
      "description": "Semantic version of the FAQ entry."
    },
    "metadata": {
      "type": "object",
      "properties": {
        "created_at": {
          "type": "string",
          "format": "date-time",
          "description": "ISO‑8601 timestamp of creation."
        },
        "updated_at": {
          "type": "string",
          "format": "date-time",
          "description": "ISO‑8601 timestamp of last update."
        },
        "source": {
          "type": "string",
          "maxLength": 256,
          "description": "Human‑readable source reference (e.g., document name)."
        }
      },
      "additionalProperties": false,
      "description": "Optional audit fields."
    }
  },
  "additionalProperties": false
}
```

*Key points*  

* **question** and **answer** are mandatory; all other fields are optional.  
* **categories** and **tags** are arrays that enforce uniqueness.  
* **confidence_score** is a floating‑point value constrained to the inclusive range `[0.0, 1.0]`.  
* **version** follows Semantic Versioning [2] and optionally stores a UUID revision identifier for immutable tracking.  
* **metadata** supplies audit timestamps and an optional source reference.  

---

## 5. Validation Rules  

| Rule ID | Target | Condition | Action on Violation |
|--------|--------|-----------|---------------------|
| V‑001 | `question` | `type = string` & `1 ≤ length ≤ 1024` | Reject entry; log “Invalid question length”. |
| V‑002 | `answer` | `type = string` & `1 ≤ length ≤ 8192` | Reject entry; log “Invalid answer length”. |
| V‑003 | `categories` | If present, each item must be a non‑empty string ≤ 128 chars; array must be unique. | Reject entry; log “Malformed categories”. |
| V‑004 | `tags` | If present, each item must match regex `^[a-z0-9_-]{1,64}$`; array must be unique. | Reject entry; log “Invalid tag format”. |
| V‑005 | `confidence_score` | If present, numeric value must be `0.0 ≤ value ≤ 1.0`. | Set to `null` and flag “Score out of range”. |
| V‑006 | `version` | Must contain `major`, `minor`, `patch` integers ≥ 0. `revision_id` (if present) must be a valid UUID. | Reject entry; log “Version object malformed”. |
| V‑007 | `metadata.created_at` / `metadata.updated_at` | If present, must be RFC 3339 date‑time strings. | Reject entry; log “Invalid timestamp”. |
| V‑008 | Overall object | No additional properties beyond those defined. | Reject entry; log “Unexpected property”. |

**Implementation tip** – Use a JSON‑Schema validator library (e.g., `ajv` for Node.js, `jsonschema` for Python) and enforce the above rules as part of the ingestion pipeline.  

---  

## 6. Versioning Strategy  

1. **Semantic versioning** (`major.minor.patch`) is stored per FAQ entry.  
2. **Increment rules**  
   * **Patch** – Minor textual edits, typo fixes, or confidence‑score updates.  
   * **Minor** – Adding new tags, categories, or supplemental answer sections without breaking existing consumer expectations.  
   * **Major** – Re‑writing the answer, removing a category, or changing the question phrasing.  
3. **Revision ID** – A UUID generated for every patch‑level change; immutable and useful for audit logs.  
4. **Change propagation** – Downstream services should reload only entries whose `version` tuple differs from the cached copy.  

---

# AI FAQ Receptionist – Client Onboarding Playbook  
*Version 1.0 – 2026‑07‑01*  

---

## 1. Purpose  

Provide a repeatable, end‑to‑end process for onboarding a new client onto the **AI FAQ Receptionist** product. The playbook defines every activity, the responsible owner, required inputs/outputs, acceptance criteria, and a visual timeline. It is intended for use by the Project Management Office (PMO), Sales Engineering, Content, Data, and Support teams.  

---

## 2. Scope  

- **In‑Scope:** All activities from the moment a client signs the Master Services Agreement (MSA) through the first 30 days of live operation.  
- **Out‑of‑Scope:** Long‑term account expansion, custom integration development beyond the standard API, and post‑launch product enhancements.  

---

## 3. Roles & Responsibilities  

| Role | Primary Owner | Key Responsibilities |
|------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Project Manager** | Iris (PM) | Overall schedule, risk tracking, stakeholder communication, final sign‑off. |
| **Client Success Lead** | CS Lead | MSA receipt, client liaison, kickoff meeting coordination. |
| **Data Engineer** | Data Eng | Secure data transfer, schema validation, import into AI platform. |
| **Content Specialist** | Content Lead | FAQ extraction, authoring, quality review, taxonomy tagging. |
| **Sales Engineer** | SE Lead | Demo environment setup, walkthrough rehearsal, demo delivery. |
| **Support Engineer** | Support Lead | Go‑live checklist execution, monitoring configuration, 30‑day support hand‑off. |
| **Legal / Compliance** | Legal Lead | Data‑privacy compliance verification, GDPR/CCPA checklists. |

*All owners must acknowledge task completion in the shared project tracker (e.g., Jira, Asana).*

---

## 4. Timeline & Critical Path  

| Day | Milestone | Owner | Dependencies |
|-----|-----------|-------|--------------|
| **0** | **Kickoff & MSA Confirmation** | CS Lead | – |
| **1‑3** | **Data Collection Request Sent** | CS Lead | – |
| **4‑7** | **Client Provides Raw FAQ Data** | Client | – |
| **8‑10** | **Data Validation & Ingestion** | Data Engineer | Data received |
| **11‑14** | **FAQ Authoring (Draft)** | Content Specialist | Data ingested |
| **15‑16** | **Content Review & Approval** | CS Lead + Client SME | Draft ready |
| **17‑18** | **Demo Environment Build** | Sales Engineer | Approved FAQ set |
| **19‑20** | **Internal Demo Rehearsal** | Sales Engineer | Demo built |
| **21** | **Client Demo & Sign‑off** | Sales Engineer | Rehearsal complete |
| **22‑23** | **Go‑Live Checklist Execution** | Support Engineer | Demo sign‑off |
| **24‑30** | **Post‑Launch Monitoring & Support** | Support Engineer | Go‑live complete |
| **31** | **Project Close‑out & Handover** | Project Manager | All prior tasks completed |

**Critical Path:**  
Data Collection → Data Validation → FAQ Authoring → Demo Preparation → Go‑Live Checklist → Post‑Launch Support. Any delay on a critical‑path task pushes the overall onboarding timeline.

---

## 5. Detailed Playbook Steps  

### 5.1 Kickoff & MSA Confirmation  
1. **Schedule Kickoff (Day 0)** – CS Lead sends calendar invite to client stakeholders (Project Sponsor, SME, IT).  
2. **Review MSA** – Legal Lead confirms signed agreement, data‑processing clauses, and any jurisdiction‑specific requirements.  
3. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - MSA signed and stored in the secure repository.  
   - Kickoff meeting held; agenda and minutes recorded.  

### 5.2 Data Collection  
1. **Send Data Request (Day 1‑3)** – CS Lead provides a templated data request email (see Appendix A) that lists required formats (CSV, JSON, or plain‑text) and naming conventions.  
2. **Client Upload** – Client uploads files to the encrypted client portal (SFTP or SharePoint).  
3. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - All required files received (minimum: FAQ source, product taxonomy, contact metadata).  
   - Files pass checksum verification (SHA‑256) and are free of personally identifiable information (PII).  

### 5.3 Data Validation & Ingestion  
1. **Schema Validation** – Data Engineer runs automated validation scripts (Python `pandas` + `jsonschema`).  
2. **PII Scrubbing** – Apply regex‑based redaction for email addresses, phone numbers, etc.  
3. **Import** – Load cleaned data into the AI FAQ platform via the REST API (`/v1/faq/import`).  
4. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - Validation logs show 0 errors.  
   - Import API returns `200 OK` with `import_id`.  

### 5.4 FAQ Authoring  
1. **Draft Generation** – Content Specialist uses the platform’s “auto‑author” feature to generate initial Q&A pairs.  
2. **Manual Review** – Subject‑matter experts (SMEs) from the client review each entry for accuracy, tone, and completeness.  
3. **Tagging** – Apply taxonomy tags (e.g., “Billing”, “Technical Support”) per the client’s classification guide.  
4. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - ≥ 95 % of auto‑generated FAQs approved without major edits.  
   - All FAQs have at least one taxonomy tag.  

### 5.5 Demo Preparation  
1. **Environment Build** – Sales Engineer clones the staging environment, loads the approved FAQ set, and configures branding (logo, colors).  
2. **Rehearsal** – Internal demo run with PM and CS Lead to verify flow, timing, and answer accuracy.  
3. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - Demo runs end‑to‑end without errors.  
   - Response latency ≤ 2 seconds for the top‑10 most‑asked questions.  

### 5.6 Client Demo & Sign‑off  
1. **Demo Delivery (Day 21)** – Conduct live demo with client stakeholders, capture feedback.  
2. **Sign‑off** – Client signs the “Demo Acceptance Form” confirming satisfaction with answer quality and UI.  
3. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - Signed form stored in the project folder.  
   - No open critical issues (> P1) remain.  

### 5.7 Go‑Live Checklist  
| ✅ | Item | Owner | Due |
|----|------|-------|-----|
| ☐ | Production API endpoint configured | Support Engineer | Day 22 |
| ☐ | Monitoring alerts (CPU, latency, error rate) enabled | Support Engineer | Day 22 |
| ☐ | Backup schedule (daily snapshot) verified | Data Engineer | Day 22 |
| ☐ | Client “Launch” communication sent (email template) | CS Lead | Day 23 |
| ☐ | Final QA test (5 random live queries) passed | Support Engineer | Day 23 |
| ☐ | Incident response run‑book updated with client contact info | Support Engineer | Day 23 |
| ☐ | All acceptance criteria from previous steps met | Project Manager | Day 24 |

**Acceptance Criteria** – All checklist items marked ✅ and signed off by the respective owners.  

### 5.8 Post‑Launch Monitoring & Support (Days 24‑30)  
1. **Live Traffic Review** – Support Engineer reviews first‑day logs for error spikes.  
2. **Client Check‑in (Day 27)** – CS Lead holds a 30‑minute call to capture any usability concerns.  
3. **Issue Resolution** – Any P1/P2 tickets are resolved within 24 hours.  
4. **Handover Document** – Compile a “Post‑Launch Support Pack” (access credentials, escalation matrix).  
5. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - No unresolved P1 tickets after Day 30.  
   - Handover pack delivered and acknowledged by the client’s support team.  

### 5.9 Project Close‑out (Day 31)  
1. **Final Report** – PM prepares a one‑page summary (timeline, variances, lessons learned).  
2. **Archive** – All artefacts (emails, checklists, logs) stored in the project repository with read‑only access.  
3. **Acceptance Criteria**  
   - Client signs the “Project Completion Sign‑off”.  
   - Archive verified by Legal Lead.  

---

## 6. Acceptance Criteria Summary  

| Phase | Criteria | Owner |
|-------|----------|-------|
| Kickoff | MSA signed, kickoff minutes recorded | CS Lead |
| Data Collection | All files received, checksum OK, PII removed | Data Engineer |
| Authoring | ≥ 95 % auto‑FAQ approved, taxonomy applied | Content Specialist |
| Demo | Latency ≤ 2 s, no errors, client sign‑off | Sales Engineer |
| Go‑Live | All checklist items ✅, production endpoint live | Support Engineer |
| Post‑Launch | No open P1 tickets, hand‑over pack acknowledged | Support Engineer |
| Close‑out | Completion sign‑off, archive verified | Project Manager |

---

## 7. Dependencies & Risks  

| Dependency | Impact if Delayed | Mitigation |
|------------|-------------------|------------|
| Client data delivery (Day 4‑7) | Shifts entire timeline | CS Lead sends reminder at Day 5; offer secure file‑transfer assistance. |
| Content review (Day 15‑16) | Demo cannot be built | Provide a “rapid‑review” window (2 hours) with client SME; prioritize high‑traffic FAQs. |
| Production API configuration (Day 22) | Go‑live postponed | Pre‑configure a sandbox endpoint; use feature flag to toggle live mode. |
| Monitoring alert setup (Day 22) | Post‑launch incidents unnoticed | Use default alert templates; test alerts during internal QA. |

| Competitor | Plan(s) | Our Comparable Tier | Price Difference |
|------------|---------|--------------------|------------------|
| CallBird AI | $99‑$499 (tiered) | **Starter** ($99) / **Growth** ($199) / **Enterprise** ($399) | Overlaps – CallBird’s low tier matches our Starter price; higher tiers are more expensive than our Enterprise. |
| Dialzara | $79‑$149 (tiered) | **Starter** ($99) | Dialzara’s low tier ($79) is cheaper than our Starter; its high tier ($149) sits between our Starter and Growth. |
| NextPhone AI | $199 (flat) | **Growth** ($199) | Exact price match for Growth tier; features comparable (unlimited calls, full feature set). |
| My AI Front Desk | $65‑$195 (tiered) | **Starter** ($99) / **Growth** ($199) | Lower‑priced tiers undercut our Starter; top tier ($195) slightly below our Growth tier. |
| ai‑receptionist.com | $14 – $199 – up to $2,500 | **Starter** ($99) aligns with their $99 small‑business plan; their $199 team plan matches our Growth tier; enterprise pricing far exceeds our Enterprise tier. |

---

## 5. Gap Analysis
| Dimension | Where We Are Strong | Where We Are Weak / Gap |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Price Competitiveness** | *Growth* tier ($199) matches the market‑leader flat‑rate (NextPhone) and undercuts higher‑priced competitors (CallBird Enterprise, Ruby Receptionists). | *Starter* tier is slightly higher than the cheapest pure‑AI options (Dialzara $79, My AI Front Desk $65). |
| **Feature Coverage** | Unlimited calls, core integrations, multi‑user access – on par with NextPhone and CallBird mid‑tiers. | No hybrid human‑agent option; competitors like Smith.ai and Abby Connect offer AI+human blends at $99‑$399, which could appeal to customers desiring a safety net. |
| **Pricing Transparency** | Simple tiered pricing (3 tiers) – easier for SMB decision‑makers than many tiered ranges. | Some competitors provide a wide range (e.g., CallBird $99‑$499) which can cause confusion; we should ensure clear feature mapping per tier. |
| **Market Positioning** | Competitive with flat‑rate leaders; positioned as cost‑effective AI‑only solution. | Lacks a low‑cost entry point (< $80) that could attract solo entrepreneurs. |

---

## 6. Recommendations
1. **Introduce a *Lite* entry tier** (≈ $79 /month) with core AI call handling and limited integrations to capture price‑sensitive solo entrepreneurs (directly competing with Dialzara and My AI Front Desk).
2. **Add an optional *Human‑Assist* add‑on** (e.g., $50 /month) that routes overflow to human agents. This would address the hybrid‑model gap exploited by Smith.ai and Abby Connect.
3. **Clarify feature‑to‑price mapping** on our website (e.g., a matrix showing which features are unlocked at each tier) to reduce buyer confusion and differentiate from CallBird’s broad pricing bands.
4. **Monitor pricing trends** quarterly (especially for emerging low‑cost entrants) to ensure our tiers remain competitive without eroding margin.
5. **Bundle multi‑year contracts** with a modest discount (5‑10%) to increase ARR stability, similar to how NextPhone promotes a flat‑rate model.

---

# AI FAQ Receptionist – Data Privacy & Compliance Checklist  
*Prepared for Project Team – Security Specialist (💀 Hades)*

## 1. Scope & Objective  
This checklist validates that the AI‑driven FAQ Receptionist service complies with **EU GDPR**, **California CCPA**, and other applicable privacy statutes (e.g., **UK‑UK GDPR**, **Australia Privacy Act**, **Brazil LGPD**). It covers the full data lifecycle – from collection through storage, processing, user‑rights handling, and incident response. Each item is **actionable**, includes a **severity rating**, and references the exact source (regulation article, internal config file, or policy document) used to derive the requirement.

> **Note:** Where a concrete artefact (e.g., `config.yaml`, `privacy_policy.md`) is referenced, the path is relative to the workspace root. If a file does not yet exist, the checklist recommends its creation.

---

## 2. Checklist Items  

| # | Category | Requirement (Regulation) | Current Artefact / Location | Action | Owner | Deadline | Severity | Remediation |
|---|----------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|--------|-------|----------|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Consent & Lawful Basis** | GDPR Art. 6(1)(a) – consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous. | `frontend/src/ConsentBanner.jsx` – displays generic “Accept Cookies”. | Replace banner with a **granular consent UI** that records consent per data purpose (e.g., analytics, personalization). Store consent receipt in `data/consent_log.json`. | Front‑end Lead | 2026‑08‑15 | **High** | Implement consent manager library (e.g., OneTrust) and persist logs. |
| 2 | **Consent & Lawful Basis** | CCPA §1798.140 – opt‑out of sale of personal information must be honored. | No explicit “Do Not Sell My Info” link. | Add a persistent “Do Not Sell My Info” link on the FAQ UI, wired to `api/v1/optout` endpoint. | UI/UX Lead | 2026‑08‑01 | **Medium** | Endpoint must set a flag in `db/user_optout` and prevent any downstream data‑sale pipelines. |
| 3 | **Data Minimisation** | GDPR Art. 5(1)(c) – collect only data necessary for the stated purpose. | `api/v1/submit_question` logs full IP, User‑Agent, and raw question text. | Redact IP to **city‑level** before storage; store only hashed User‑Agent. Update logging middleware (`middleware/logging.py`). | Backend Lead | 2026‑08‑10 | **High** | Use `geoip2` to map IP → city; hash User‑Agent with SHA‑256. |
| 4 | **Purpose Limitation** | GDPR Art. 5(1)(b) – data must not be processed for incompatible purposes. | No documented purpose statement. | Draft a **Data Processing Purpose Register** (`docs/purpose_register.md`) listing: (a) FAQ answer generation, (b) service improvement, (c) analytics. Ensure no secondary uses (e.g., marketing) without new consent. | Compliance Officer | 2026‑08‑20 | **Medium** | Align all data pipelines to the register; lock down any ad‑tech integrations. |
| 5 | **Retention & Deletion** | GDPR Art. 5(1)(e) – retain data no longer than necessary. | Retention policy not defined; DB tables keep records indefinitely. | Create a **Retention Schedule** (`config/retention.yaml`) – e.g., raw questions → 30 days, anonymised embeddings → 180 days. Implement a nightly purge job (`jobs/purge_retention.py`). | DevOps Lead | 2026‑09‑01 | **High** | Verify purge logs in `logs/retention_purge.log`. |
| 6 | **Data Subject Access Request (DSAR)** | GDPR Art. 15 – provide copy of personal data on request. | No DSAR endpoint. | Deploy `api/v1/dsar` – authenticates via OAuth2, returns JSON of all stored records for the user. Log request in `audit/dsar_requests.log`. | Backend Lead | 2026‑09‑15 | **Critical** | Ensure response time ≤ 30 days; encrypt payload with TLS 1.3. |
| 7 | **Right to Erasure** | GDPR Art. 17 – delete personal data upon request. | No deletion API. | Add `api/v1/delete_user_data` – triggers cascade delete of user‑linked records. Record action in `audit/erasure.log`. | Backend Lead | 2026‑09‑15 | **Critical** | Verify that backups also purge the data within 7 days. |
| 8 | **Right to Restrict Processing** | GDPR Art. 18 – allow users to limit processing. | Not supported. | Extend DSAR UI to include “Restrict Processing” toggle; store flag in `db/user_restriction`. Ensure downstream analytics skip restricted users. | Front‑end Lead | 2026‑09‑30 | **Medium** | Add unit tests for restriction enforcement. |
| 9 | **Data Encryption – In‑Transit** | GDPR Art. 32 – pseudonymisation & encryption of data in transit. | API served over HTTP on internal dev network. | Enforce **HTTPS** with TLS 1.3 across all environments. Update `nginx/conf.d/faq.conf` to redirect HTTP → HTTPS. | Infra Lead | 2026‑08‑05 | **Critical** | Use Let's Encrypt certificates; scan with `sslscan` to verify. |
|10 | **Data Encryption – At‑Rest** | GDPR Art. 32 – encryption of personal data at rest. | PostgreSQL tables stored unencrypted. | Enable **Transparent Data Encryption (TDE)** via `pgcrypto` or cloud‑provider KMS. Store encryption keys in `vault/keys/faq_kms`. | DB Admin | 2026‑09‑10 | **Critical** | Verify encryption with `SELECT pg_is_in_recovery();` and key rotation policy. |
|11 | **Key Management** | GDPR Art. 32(1)(b) – secure key lifecycle. | Keys stored in plain‑text file `config/keys.txt`. | Migrate keys to **HashiCorp Vault** (`vault/faq/`). Restrict access to Vault policies. Rotate keys every 90 days. | Security Engineer | 2026‑09‑20 | **High** | Document rotation procedure in `ops/key_rotation.md`. |
|12 | **Access Control** | CCPA §1798.150 – reasonable security measures. | IAM roles not documented; many services share a single service‑account. | Implement **least‑privilege IAM**: create separate service accounts for UI, API, and analytics. Grant only required DB permissions. Record in `iam/role_matrix.md`. | Cloud Admin | 2026‑08‑25 | **High** | Run `gcloud iam roles describe` to verify. |
|13 | **Audit Logging** | GDPR Art. 30 – maintain records of processing activities. | Limited logging; only error logs captured. | Deploy **centralised audit log** (`elastic/faq_audit`) capturing: data access, DSAR, erasure, consent changes. Retain logs 12 months. | DevOps Lead | 2026‑09‑05 | **Medium** | Ensure logs are immutable (write‑once). |
|14 | **Vendor Management** | GDPR Art. 28 – processor agreements. | Third‑party AI model hosted on `openai.com` (no DPA attached). | Obtain **Data Processing Addendum (DPA)** from OpenAI or switch to a self‑hosted model. Record agreement in `contracts/openai_dpa.pdf`. | Legal Counsel | 2026‑10‑01 | **Critical** | Until DPA is secured, disable any data‑export features. |
|15 | **Privacy Notice** | GDPR Art. 12‑14 – transparent information to data subjects. | `docs/privacy_policy.md` outdated (last edited 2022). | Rewrite privacy notice to include: data categories, purposes, retention, rights, contact details. Publish at `/privacy`. Link from every UI page. | Content Lead | 2026‑08‑15 | **Medium** | Review by Legal; add versioning (`v1.0‑2026‑08`). |
|16 | **Children’s Data** | GDPR Art. 8 – special protections for children < 16. | No age verification. | Add **age‑gate** before FAQ usage; if user < 16, require parental consent (recorded in `data/parental_consent.json`). | Front‑end Lead | 2026‑09‑30 | **High** | Log consent and provide opt‑out mechanism. |
|17 | **Cross‑Border Transfers** | GDPR Art. 44‑50 – adequacy or SCCs required. | Model inference calls to `us-east-1` AWS region. | Verify **Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs)** are in place for EU→US transfers. Document in `contracts/scc_us.pdf`. | Legal Counsel | 2026‑09‑15 | **High** | If SCCs missing, route inference through EU‑located endpoint. |
|18 | **Incident Response** | GDPR Art. 33 – breach notification within 72 h. | No breach playbook. | Draft **Incident Response Playbook** (`ops/incident_response.md`) covering detection, containment, notification (to DPO & authorities). Conduct tabletop drill. | Security Lead | 2026‑10‑15 | **Critical** | Assign DPO contact: `dpo@company.com`. |
|19 | **Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA)** | GDPR Art. 35 – required for high‑risk processing. | No DPIA performed. | Conduct DPIA focusing on AI‑generated answers, profiling, and cross‑border transfers. Store report in `docs/dpia_faq.pdf`. | Privacy Officer | 2026‑09‑30 | **Critical** | Review by DPO before go‑live. |
|20 | **CCPA “Do Not Sell” Enforcement** | CCPA §1798.150(b) – must honor opt‑out across all data flows. | Advertising SDK still receives user ID. | Audit all third‑party SDKs; disable data sharing for opted‑out users. Document audit in `audit/ccpa_optout.md`. | Security Engineer | 2026‑09‑10 | **Medium** | Re‑run quarterly. |

# Add‑On Feature Catalog for AI FAQ Receptionist

**Prepared for:** AUTO‑104 – Add‑On Feature Catalog
**Prepared by:** Hermes (Market Intel Lead)
**Date:** 2026‑07‑01

---

## Overview
The AI FAQ Receptionist is a 24/7 virtual receptionist that already provides call routing, multi‑language support (currently English only), and a suite of core features.  Organizations often need extra capabilities to tailor the solution to specific business requirements.  The table below catalogs five high‑value add‑on features, describing what each does, its likely pricing impact, and the expected implementation effort.

---

| # | Add‑On Feature | Brief Description | Pricing Impact* | Implementation Effort** | Source |
|---|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|------------------------|--------|
| 1 | **Multi‑Language Support** | Extends the AI receptionist to understand and speak additional languages beyond the default English. Enables businesses to serve international callers without hiring multilingual staff. | Additional subscription charge (often a fixed monthly fee per extra language). Exact price not disclosed; would be negotiated with vendor. | **Medium** – Requires language model licensing, integration testing, and UI localisation. | [2] |
| 2 | **Sentiment Analysis** | Analyzes caller tone and sentiment in real time, flagging dissatisfied callers for priority handling or providing analytics on overall customer satisfaction. | Typically an add‑on tier or per‑minute charge for the analytics processing. No public price; assumed to increase the monthly bill. | **Medium** – Leverages existing voice analytics engine; needs configuration and dashboards. | [1] |
| 3 | **Custom Branding / Personality** | Allows businesses to customise the receptionist’s voice, greeting script, and visual assets (logo, colors) to match brand identity. Supports custom personality settings for a tailored caller experience. | One‑time setup fee plus possible ongoing branding maintenance fee. Pricing not publicly listed; treated as an extra service charge. | **Low** – Mostly a configuration task in the admin portal; may involve uploading assets and setting parameters. | [3] |
| 4 | **Analytics Integration** | Connects call‑level data (volume, routing, transcript, sentiment) to external BI tools or CRM systems for deeper business insights. Includes AI Call Analytics and Voice Analytics dashboards. | May require an advanced services subscription; pricing varies by integration complexity. | **High** – Requires API development, data mapping, and possibly custom connectors. | [1] |
| 5 | **Advanced Voice Interface** | Provides richer voice interactions such as natural‑language scheduling, dynamic prompts, and voice‑activated workflows (e.g., “book an appointment”). Enhances the receptionist’s conversational capabilities. | Additional per‑minute or tiered pricing for advanced AI speech processing. No explicit rates disclosed; assumes higher usage cost. | **High** – Involves custom voice model tuning, testing, and possibly additional licensing. | [1],[3] |

---

### Notes on Pricing Impact
- The vendor (Imagicle) does not publish exact dollar amounts for these add‑ons in the public sources. All pricing impact statements are therefore **illustrative** and should be confirmed with the sales team.
- Where the source mentions a pricing model (e.g., pay‑per‑minute or fixed‑price plans) – see Source [2] – we infer that add‑ons will follow a similar model, but the exact fee per add‑on is not provided.

### Implementation Effort Scale
- **Low** – Configuration‑only changes that can be made through the web admin console without code changes.
- **Medium** – Requires some development effort such as enabling language packs, configuring analytics modules, or integrating third‑party services.
- **High** – Involves substantial custom development, API work, or model training.

---

## Recommendations
Based on the typical needs of SMBs deploying the AI FAQ Receptionist, the **most impactful add‑ons** are:
1. **Multi‑Language Support** – Opens new markets with relatively modest effort.
2. **Analytics Integration** – Provides measurable ROI through data‑driven decisions.
3. **Custom Branding** – Enhances brand consistency with low effort.

These three should be prioritized for most customers, followed by Sentiment Analysis and Advanced Voice Interface as growth‑stage enhancements.

## 3. Pricing Rationale  

| Source | Observed market range | How we positioned each tier |
|--------|----------------------|-----------------------------|
| **[1] Voksha AI Receptionist Pricing Guide 2026** – “Budget Tier ($29‑$49/mo)”, “Mid Tier ($50‑$150/mo)”, “Premium Tier ($200+/mo)” | $29‑$49 (budget), $50‑$150 (mid), $200+ (premium) | **Basic** sits at the low‑end of the budget tier ($29) to attract price‑sensitive SMBs. **Pro** lands in the middle of the mid‑tier ($99) – a price point also used by ai‑receptionist.com for its small‑business plan [2]. **Enterprise** aligns with the premium tier ($199) and mirrors the “teams” plan of ai‑receptionist.com [2]. |
| **[2] ai‑receptionist.com Pricing Guide** – $14 (individual), $99 (small business), $199 (teams) | $14‑$199 | We adopt the $99 and $199 levels directly, because they are proven “real‑world” prices for feature‑rich plans. The $14 plan is too limited for FAQ bots (only 60 minutes of voice), so we start the Basic tier at $29. |
| **[3] GetAIRA.io Blog** – $24.95/mo (30 calls), $29/mo (60 minutes), $49/mo (250 minutes) | $25‑$49 for entry‑level plans | Confirms that a $29 flat‑rate is competitive for a flat‑rate, query‑based offering. |

**Why flat‑rate?**  
All three sources stress that “flat‑rate (fixed monthly fee) is the simplest and most predictable model” [1]. FAQ bots typically handle short, predictable queries, so a flat‑rate eliminates surprise overage charges and simplifies budgeting for customers.

## 1. Purpose

| **Objection** | Typical phrasing a prospect uses. |

| **Proof Items** | Tangible artefacts (case study, metric, demo) that can be shared on‑the‑fly. |

# AI FAQ Receptionist – Pricing Tier Sheet  
*Prepared for Sales Enablement – ready to copy into a sales deck or CRM.*

## 1. Tier Overview  

| Tier | Monthly Fee* | Base Interactions Included† | Per‑Interaction Cost‡ (beyond base) | Typical Use‑Case |
|------|--------------|----------------------------|--------------------------------------|------------------|
| **Basic** | **$29 /mo** | 100 calls / messages per month | **$1.50 / call** | Small shops, freelancers, solo consultants – low‑volume inbound traffic. |
| **Pro** | **$99 /mo** | 500 calls / messages per month | **$0.90 / call** | Growing SMBs (dentists, legal firms, e‑commerce) that need appointment booking & CRM sync. |
| **Enterprise** | **$299 /mo** | Unlimited (up to 5 k calls) | **Included** (no extra per‑call charge) | Multi‑location enterprises, call‑center outsourcing, regulated industries (HIPAA‑required). |

\* **Monthly Fee** is a flat‑rate, all‑features price (no hidden overage fees).  
† **Base Interactions** are the number of inbound calls or chat messages covered by the flat fee.  
‡ **Per‑Interaction Cost** applies only when the customer exceeds the base allowance. Prices are rounded to the nearest cent.  

### Sources  

| Source | Key Data Used |
|--------|---------------|
| **[1] Voksha AI Receptionist Pricing Guide 2026** | Budget tier $29‑$49 /mo, Mid tier $50‑$150 /mo, Premium $200+ /mo (price bands)【1】 |
| **[3] CallBird AI Receptionist Pricing Guide 2026** | Budget $29‑$79 /mo, Mid $99‑$197 /mo, Enterprise $299‑$500+ /mo【3】 |
| **[2] AI‑Receptionist.com Pricing** | $14 /mo (individual), $99 /mo (small business), $199 /mo (team) – used to derive realistic per‑minute cost assumptions【2】 |

---  

## 2. Cost‑Basis Calculations  

| Assumption | Calculation | Result |
|------------|-------------|--------|
| **Average call duration** | 3.5 minutes (industry average cited in Voksha guide)【1】 | — |
| **Per‑minute cost (illustrative)** | $14 /mo for 60 min → $0.233 / min | $0.233 / min |
| **Per‑interaction cost (illustrative)** | $0.233 × 3.5 min ≈ $0.82 / call | $0.82 / call |
| **Rounded per‑interaction cost for tiers** | Basic: $0.82 × 1.8 ≈ $1.50 (adds margin for low‑volume pricing) | $1.50 / call |
| | Pro: $0.82 × 1.1 ≈ $0.90 (mid‑tier discount) | $0.90 / call |
| | Enterprise: unlimited – price baked into flat fee | — |

*All per‑interaction figures are **illustrative**; they are derived from publicly‑available subscription prices and the average call length shown in the Voksha guide. The rounding provides clean, market‑friendly numbers.*

---  

## 3. Optional Add‑Ons  

| Add‑On | Monthly Price | Description | Applicability |
|--------|---------------|-------------|---------------|
| **Custom Integration** | $50 /mo | API / webhook connection to bespoke CRM, ERP, or ticketing system. | Pro, Enterprise |
| **Dedicated Account Manager** | $100 /mo | Single point of contact, quarterly business reviews, SLA‑backed support. | Enterprise |
| **HIPAA / GDPR Compliance** | $150 /mo | Encrypted data handling, audit logs, compliance reporting. | Enterprise (required for healthcare, finance) |
| **Additional Voice Minutes** | $0.20 / min | For flat‑rate plans that exceed allocated minutes (rare for Enterprise). | Basic, Pro |

*Add‑ons are **stackable**; the total monthly price is the sum of the base tier fee plus any selected add‑ons.*

---  

## 4. Quick‑Reference Pricing Matrix  

| Tier | Monthly Fee | Base Interactions | Over‑age Cost | Add‑Ons (selected) | Total (example) |
|------|-------------|-------------------|---------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| **Basic** | $29 | 100 | $1.50 / call | + Additional Voice Minutes (200 min) → $40 | **$69** |
| **Pro** | $99 | 500 | $0.90 / call | + Custom Integration → $50 | **$149** |
| **Enterprise** | $299 | Unlimited | — | + Dedicated AM $100 + HIPAA $150 → $250 | **$549** |

*The “Total (example)” column shows a typical configuration a sales rep might quote for a prospect. Adjust add‑ons per customer need.*

---  

## 5. How to Use This Sheet  

1. **Identify the prospect’s monthly interaction volume** (calls + chat messages).  
2. **Select the tier** that covers the base volume.  
3. **Calculate any over‑age cost** using the per‑interaction rate.  
4. **Add optional services** the prospect requires (e.g., compliance, custom integration).  
5. **Sum the line items** for the final monthly price to present in the proposal.  

*All calculations can be reproduced in a simple spreadsheet using the formulas below.*  

### Spreadsheet Formula Cheat‑Sheet  

| Cell | Formula (Excel/Google Sheets) | Explanation |
|------|-------------------------------|-------------|
| `B2` | `=IF(A2<=100,29, IF(A2<=500,99,299))` | Tier fee based on total interactions (A2). |
| `C2` | `=MAX(0, A2-100)*1.5` | Over‑age cost for Basic tier (replace 100/1.5 for other tiers). |
| `D2` | `=SUM(B2,C2,E2:G2)` | Grand total (add‑on columns E‑G). |
| `E2` | `=IF(IncludeCustomIntegration,50,0)` | Custom integration add‑on. |
| `F2` | `=IF(IncludeDedicatedAM,100,0)` | Dedicated account manager add‑on. |
| `G2` | `=IF(IncludeHIPAA,150,0)` | HIPAA compliance add‑on. |

*Replace `A2` with the prospect’s projected interaction count and toggle the boolean flags for add‑ons.*

---  

## 6. Assumptions & Limitations  

| # | Assumption | Reasoning |
|---|------------|-----------|
| **A1** | Average call length = **3.5 minutes** (industry average) | Directly cited in Voksha guide【1】. |
| **A2** | Per‑minute cost derived from **$14 /mo for 60 min** (AI‑Receptionist.com) | Provides a concrete per‑minute price to anchor per‑interaction calculations【2】. |
| **A3** | Per‑interaction cost rounded up for Basic tier to **$1.50** to reflect typical low‑volume pricing premium. | Aligns with market practice of higher per‑call rates for budget plans. |
| **A4** | Unlimited interactions for Enterprise tier are **≤ 5 000 calls** per month (practical ceiling for most large customers). | CallBird guide lists “up to 5 k calls” as a realistic cap for enterprise plans【3】. |
| **A5** | Add‑on pricing is **flat monthly** (no per‑use fees) to simplify quoting. | Common in SaaS add‑on models; no source conflict. |
| **A6** | No hidden fees (e.g., setup, termination) are included in the sheet. | All prices are presented as **monthly recurring**; any one‑time fees must be negotiated separately. |

All figures that are **not directly quoted** from the source material are clearly labeled as *illustrative* and derived from the assumptions above.
